Examination: Teeth 15, 44, and 46 are missing, with well-healed gingiva, adequate keratinized gingiva, and good alveolar ridge form without significant depression. Oral hygiene is average. Normal mouth opening and temporomandibular joint function. Teeth 26, 34, and 36 have sutures in place with well-healed wounds.
Radiographic Examination: Teeth 15 and 46 show adequate bone volume and quality. Tooth 44 shows adequate bone quality but insufficient volume.
Diagnosis: Teeth 15, 26, 34, 36, 44, and 46: dental arch defect.
Treatment Plan: 
Treatment Recommendations: Implant-supported prostheses for teeth 15, 44, and 46. Suture removal for teeth 34 and 36.
Management: For teeth 15, 44, and 46, informed consent was obtained, and the patient signed the consent form for implant restoration. After routine disinfection, local infiltration anesthesia with 4% articaine was administered. Circular incisions were made at the crest of the alveolar ridge for tooth 15, and horizontal incisions for teeth 44 and 46. Full-thickness flaps were elevated, and osteotomies were prepared to the planned depth. A BEGO implant (3.75x10mm) was placed in tooth 15 with a healing abutment (4.5x5mm), and BEGO implants (3.75x10mm and 4.5x10mm) were placed in teeth 44 and 46 with cover screws. Tension-free suturing was performed, and radiographic verification was satisfactory. Sutures were removed from teeth 34 and 36 after disinfection.
Medical Instructions: Return for follow-up if any discomfort occurs. Avoid brushing or rinsing for 24 hours. Begin eating 2 hours post-procedure, avoiding hot foods.
Remarks:

Management:

2. Implant placement using guided surgery technique.

Medical Instructions:

2. Use prescribed mouthwash to prevent infection.
3. Avoid biting hard foods on the implant site for the first few weeks.

Remarks: The patient is a suitable candidate for implant-supported prosthetics due to adequate bone quality and overall good health.

Patient Record: Follow-up consultation. Diagnosis: Orthodontic extraction.
Examination: Teeth 14, 24, 35, and 45 show proper crown eruption with visible ink-stained discoloration on occlusal surfaces, rough texture upon probing, negative percussion, and no discomfort on thermal tests.
Radiographic Examination: CBCT shows complete root development for teeth 14, 24, 35, and 45.
Diagnosis: Teeth 14, 24, 35, and 45 identified for orthodontic extraction.
Treatment Plan: Extraction of teeth 14, 24, 35, and 45.
Treatment Recommendations: Extraction of teeth 14, 24, 35, and 45.
Management: For teeth 24 and 35, after povidone-iodine disinfection, 1.7ml of 4% Articaine with epinephrine was administered for infiltration anesthesia, gingival separation was performed, teeth were luxated and extracted, and hemostasis was achieved with cotton ball pressure.
Medical Instructions: Follow-up if discomfort occurs.
Remarks:

Patient Record: Initial consultation. Diagnosis: Dental protrusion (K07.8).
Examination: The patient exhibits a Class II malocclusion with significant overjet and overbite. There is noticeable crowding in both the maxillary and mandibular arches. The upper incisors are proclined, contributing to the protrusion. Soft tissues appear healthy without signs of inflammation or infection.
Radiographic Examination: Panoramic and lateral cephalometric radiographs show impacted third molars in both jaws. The maxillary central incisors have deep pits and fissures suggestive of early carious lesions. The periodontal structures appear normal without significant bone loss.
Diagnosis: Dental protrusion (K07.8) with impacted third molars and early caries in the maxillary central incisors.
Treatment Plan: 
1. Orthodontic evaluation for comprehensive orthodontic treatment to correct malocclusion and improve aesthetics.
2. Extraction of impacted third molars to alleviate crowding and facilitate orthodontic alignment.
3. Restorative treatment for the maxillary central incisors to address early caries.
Treatment Recommendations: Regular dental check-ups every six months for monitoring and preventive care.
Management: 
1. Referral to an orthodontist for further evaluation and treatment planning.
2. Scheduling of third molar extractions by an oral surgeon.
3. Application of fluoride varnish on the maxillary central incisors to prevent progression of caries.
Medical Instructions: Maintain good oral hygiene, use fluoride toothpaste, and attend scheduled appointments for orthodontic and restorative treatments.
Remarks: The patient's age and stage of skeletal development will influence the orthodontic treatment plan. Early intervention is recommended to achieve optimal results.

Patient Record: Follow-up consultation. Diagnosis: dental arch defect (K08.100x002). Treatment: implant-supported denture restoration.
Examination: Tooth 37 site shows well-healed gingiva, adequate keratinized gingiva, good alveolar ridge form, no significant depression, and adequate gingival-occlusal distance. Oral hygiene is average. Normal mouth opening and temporomandibular joint movement.
Radiographic Examination: Tooth 37 site shows adequate bone volume and quality.
Diagnosis: Tooth 37: Dental arch defect (K08.100x002).
Treatment Plan: Tooth 37: Implant-supported denture restoration.
Treatment Recommendations: 
Management: For tooth 37, the patient was fully informed and consented to the procedure. Preoperative antibiotics were administered. Under local anesthesia, a horizontal incision was made at the alveolar ridge, full-thickness flap elevation was performed, and the implant site was prepared. An IT implant (4.1x10 mm) was placed, and a cover screw was inserted. Tension-free suturing was done, and a postoperative radiograph was taken.
Medical Instructions: Follow up if discomfort occurs. Bite on gauze for 40 minutes, avoid brushing or rinsing for 24 hours, and resume eating after 2 hours. Avoid hot foods.
Remarks:

Treatment Plan:

A BEGO implant (3.75x10mm) was placed in tooth 15 with a healing abutment (4.5x5mm), a BEGO implant (3.75x10mm) in tooth 44 with a cover screw, and a BEGO implant (4.5x10mm) in tooth 46 with a cover screw. Tension-free suturing was performed, and radiographs confirmed correct positioning. Sutures were removed from teeth 34 and 36 after disinfection.

Patient Record: Follow-up consultation. Diagnosis: Chronic gingivitis (K05.151) with pigmentation. Treatment: Full-mouth supragingival ultrasonic scaling and air polishing.
Examination: Generalized gingival swelling with easy bleeding on probing, no periodontal pockets detected. Presence of calculus and pigmentation.
Radiographic Examination: No radiographs taken.
Diagnosis: Chronic gingivitis (K05.151) with pigmentation.
Treatment Plan: Full-mouth supragingival ultrasonic scaling and air polishing.
Treatment Recommendations: 
Management: The patient was informed about the condition, treatment plan, costs, and prognosis and consented to treatment. Plaque disclosing, full-mouth ultrasonic scaling, air polishing, fluoride varnish application, and iodine glycerin were performed. Oral hygiene instruction (OHI) was provided, including brushing, flossing, and interdental brush use.
Medical Instructions: Avoid acidic foods and extreme temperatures for one week. Maintain oral hygiene with mouthwash. Regular check-ups and follow-up if discomfort occurs.
Remarks:

Patient Record: Initial consultation. Diagnosis: gingivitis (K05.2).
Examination: The patient presents with generalized gingival inflammation, redness, and bleeding upon probing. No signs of periodontal pockets or bone loss are observed. Teeth are generally well-aligned with no significant restorations or caries noted.
Radiographic Examination: Panoramic and periapical radiographs show healthy alveolar bone levels with no evidence of periodontal bone loss. All teeth appear to have normal root structures and no signs of periapical pathology.
Diagnosis: Gingivitis affecting all teeth (K05.2).
Treatment Plan: 
1. Professional dental cleaning (scaling and root planing) to remove plaque and calculus deposits.
2. Oral hygiene instruction to improve daily oral care practices.
3. Follow-up appointment in 4 weeks to assess healing and determine if further treatment is needed.
Treatment Recommendations: 
1. Use of an antimicrobial mouthwash containing chlorhexidine for 2 weeks post-cleaning.
2. Regular dental check-ups every 6 months to monitor gingival health.
Management: 
1. Supragingival scaling and root planing were performed using ultrasonic and hand instruments.
2. Polishing with pumice and a rubber cup was done to smooth the tooth surfaces.
3. Oral hygiene instructions included proper brushing technique, use of interdental brushes, and flossing.
Medical Instructions: 
1. Continue using chlorhexidine mouthwash as prescribed.
2. Maintain good oral hygiene by brushing twice daily and flossing once daily.
3. Schedule a follow-up appointment in 4 weeks for reassessment.
Remarks: The patient responded well to the initial treatment, and there was a noticeable reduction in gingival inflammation and bleeding.

Patient Record: Initial consultation. Diagnosis: tooth avulsion (K07.8).
Examination: Tooth 16 is missing, with an empty socket visible in the upper right posterior region. The surrounding gingiva appears inflamed and there is mild bleeding present. No other significant abnormalities noted in the oral cavity.
Radiographic Examination: Tooth 16 is absent in the radiograph, confirming the clinical finding of tooth avulsion. The alveolar bone around the extraction site appears intact without signs of fracture or infection.
Diagnosis: Avulsion of tooth 16 (ICD-10: K07.8).
Treatment Plan: 
1. Immediate replantation of the avulsed tooth if it is available and in good condition.
2. If replantation is not possible, consider prosthetic replacement options such as dental implants or bridges.
3. Antibiotic prophylaxis to prevent infection.
4. Referral to an endodontist for potential root canal treatment if the tooth is replanted.
Treatment Recommendations: 
1. Maintain good oral hygiene to prevent infection.
2. Use chlorhexidine mouthwash as prescribed.
3. Avoid chewing on the affected side until the tooth is replaced or healed.
Management: 
1. If the tooth is available, clean the root surface gently with saline and place it back into the socket.
2. Stabilize the replanted tooth with a splint for 2-4 weeks.
3. Prescribe antibiotics (e.g., amoxicillin) and analgesics (e.g., ibuprofen) as needed.
Medical Instructions: 
1. Keep the area clean and avoid touching the replanted tooth.
2. Return for follow-up appointments to monitor healing and assess the need for further treatment.
3. If the tooth cannot be replanted, discuss long-term prosthetic options with the patient.
Remarks: The prognosis for successful replantation depends on the duration the tooth was outside the mouth and the condition of the periodontal ligament. Early intervention is crucial for optimal outcomes.

Examination: The gingiva is red and swollen, bleeding easily upon probing, with no periodontal pockets detected. Significant calculus (++), mild pigmentation (+).
Radiographic Examination: No radiographs taken.
Diagnosis: Gingivitis (K05.105).
Treatment Plan: Full-mouth supragingival scaling.
Treatment Recommendations: 
Management: The patient was informed about the condition, treatment plan, costs, and prognosis, and consented to treatment. Full-mouth supragingival scaling and polishing were performed. Enamel sealing for caries prevention, steam eye mask application, iodine glycerin application, and oral hygiene education were provided.
Medical Instructions: Avoid acidic foods and extreme temperatures for one week. Refrain from consuming pigmented beverages like coffee, tea, and cola, and avoid smoking. Maintain oral hygiene and use mouthwash. Schedule regular follow-ups and return if any issues arise.
Remarks:

The periodontal pockets are within normal limits. Teeth show mild staining consistent with the patient's reported consumption of staining beverages.
Radiographic Examination: No significant bone loss or periodontal pocketing is observed in the radiographs. The alveolar bone levels are within normal limits.
Diagnosis: Gingival inflammation (K05.2).
Treatment Plan: 
Treatment Recommendations: 
Management: Scaling and root planing were performed to remove plaque and calculus. Oral hygiene instructions were reinforced, emphasizing proper brushing and flossing techniques. A prescription for chlorhexidine mouthwash was given for daily use for one week. The patient was scheduled for a follow-up appointment in two weeks to assess healing and further treatment needs.
Medical Instructions: Maintain good oral hygiene. Use chlorhexidine mouthwash as prescribed. Schedule follow-up appointments as recommended. Avoid smoking and reduce consumption of staining beverages.
Remarks: The patient responded well to the initial treatment, showing reduced inflammation and bleeding at the follow-up appointment.

Examination: Tooth 46: Missing with a mesiodistal gap of 8.5mm and an occlusal-gingival distance of 5mm. Alveolar ridge is resorbed, no elongation of opposing teeth. Overall oral hygiene is fair with calculus and staining present. No significant abnormalities in occlusion or joints.
Radiographic Examination: Tooth 46: Alveolar bone width of 8mm and height of 12mm.
Diagnosis: Tooth 46: Dental arch defect (K08.100×002).
Treatment Plan: Implant restoration for tooth 46.
Treatment Recommendations: 
Management: Tooth 46: The patient was fully informed and consented to the oral implant restoration procedure. Preoperative antibiotics were administered, and standard disinfection was performed. Local infiltration anesthesia with articaine was used. A horizontal incision was made at the crest of the alveolar ridge, full-thickness flap was elevated, and the site was prepared to the planned depth. A Nobel implant (5.0x10mm) was placed, and a healing abutment was attached. Tension-free suturing was done, and radiographic verification was satisfactory.
Medical Instructions: Strictly follow post-implant care instructions, take oral antibiotics, and return for follow-up if any discomfort occurs. Suture removal in two weeks.

Patient Record: Initial consultation. Diagnosis: Single arch edentulism (K08.101). Maxillary arch edentulism. Treatment: Maxillary ALL-ON-6 implant-supported prosthesis.
Examination: Teeth 11, 12, 13, 14, 15, 16, 21, 22, 23, 24, 25, 26 are missing. The gingiva at the edentulous sites is healthy, with good alveolar ridge form and adequate gingival-occlusal distance. Oral hygiene is average. Normal mouth opening and temporomandibular joint movement. No other abnormalities noted.
Radiographic Examination: Adequate bone volume and quality observed for teeth 11, 12, 13, 14, 15, 16, 21, 22, 23, 24, 25, 26.
Diagnosis: Single arch edentulism (K08.101). Maxillary arch edentulism.
Treatment Plan: Placement of ALL-ON-6 implant-supported prosthesis for teeth 11, 14, 16, 21, 24, 26.
Treatment Recommendations: 
Management: For teeth 11, 14, 16, 21, 24, 26, the patient was fully informed and consented by signing the oral implant restoration consent form. Preoperative antibiotics were administered. Standard disinfection and draping were performed. Local infiltration anesthesia with articaine was used. A horizontal incision was made along the alveolar ridge, followed by a full-thickness flap reflection. Implant sites were prepared to the planned depth, and Dentium implants were placed: 3.4*12mm for teeth 11, 21; 3.8*12mm for teeth 14, 24; 4.3*10mm for teeth 16, 26. Abutments were placed on teeth 11, 21, 14, 24, and cover screws on teeth 16, 26. Bone grafting and membrane coverage were performed. Tension-free suturing was completed, and postoperative radiographs confirmed implant positioning. The procedure lasted from 10:20 to 11:40. Impressions were taken for transitional prosthesis fabrication, and occlusion was adjusted and polished.
Medical Instructions: Strictly follow postoperative implant care instructions. Take oral antibiotics as prescribed. Return for follow-up if discomfort occurs. Suture removal in two weeks.
Remarks:

Patient Record: Follow-up consultation. Diagnosis: Moderate caries in primary teeth (K02.101). Treatment: Resin filling.
Examination: Poor overall oral hygiene with plaque accumulation at the cervical areas and carious lesions present. No tenderness on probing, cold, or percussion tests. Gingiva appears normal.
Radiographic Examination: Panoramic radiograph shows low-density images reaching the dentin layer in teeth 53, 54, 74, and 84.
Diagnosis: Moderate caries in primary teeth (K02.101).
Treatment Plan: Resin filling for the affected teeth.
Treatment Recommendations: 
Management: Caries gel was applied to soften the carious tissue, which was then removed with excavation tools. The cavity was prepared, etched with 35% phosphoric acid, and a bonding agent was applied. 3M Z350 resin was used to restore the occlusal anatomy, followed by occlusal adjustment and polishing. Teeth were cleaned, and fluoride varnish was applied to the entire mouth.
Medical Instructions: Oral hygiene education was provided, including instructions on brushing, flossing, and using interdental brushes.

Patient Record: Follow-up consultation. Diagnosis: Partial edentulism (K08.100x002).
Examination: Teeth 34, 35, and 36 are missing with a mesiodistal gap of 25.5 mm and an occlusogingival distance of 5 mm. The alveolar ridge is resorbed with no supraeruption of opposing teeth. Overall oral hygiene is fair with presence of calculus and staining. No significant occlusal or joint abnormalities noted.
Radiographic Examination: Radiographs show alveolar bone width of 5.1 mm and height of 11 mm in the area of missing teeth 34, 35, and 36.
Diagnosis: Partial edentulism of teeth 34, 35, and 36 (K08.100x002).
Treatment Plan: 
Treatment Recommendations: 
Management: Informed consent for implant restoration was obtained. Preoperative antibiotics were administered. Local anesthesia with articaine was used. A horizontal incision was made at the crest of the ridge, and a full-thickness flap was reflected. Osteotomy sites were prepared, and Dentium implants were placed: 3.4x10 mm for site 34 and 4.3x8 mm for site 36. Cover screws were placed, bone graft material was applied, and the flap was sutured with tension-free closure. Postoperative radiographs confirmed implant positioning.
Medical Instructions: Strict adherence to post-implant care instructions is required. Oral antibiotics should be taken as prescribed. Follow-up for suture removal in two weeks.
Remarks:

Tooth 25 is temporarily sealed with some soft debris accumulation.

Management: For tooth 16, the patient was informed about various restorative materials, costs, time, potential follow-up visits, and complications. The patient opted for an all-ceramic crown. Caries removal and inlay preparation were performed, followed by precise finishing and impression taking with silicone and alginate. Occlusal records and shade matching (2L1.5) were completed. For tooth 25, the temporary crown and provisional material were removed, abutment and restoration cleaned, and the restoration was tried in for fit and proximal contact. It was cemented with dual-cure resin cement, occlusion adjusted, and polished. For tooth 15, caries removal, cavity preparation, etching, drying, bonding agent application, and resin filling were performed, followed by occlusal adjustment and polishing.
Medical Instructions: Avoid biting hard objects, maintain oral hygiene, and return for follow-up.

Patient Record: Follow-up consultation. Diagnosis: Chronic pulpitis (K04.003).
Examination: Teeth 14 and 15 exhibit cervical wedge defects with pulp exposure. The mucosa in the apical region is normal, and both teeth show positive percussion.
Radiographic Examination: Teeth 14 and 15 show wedge defects with pulp exposure.
Diagnosis: Chronic pulpitis of teeth 14 and 15 (K04.003).
Treatment Plan: 
Treatment Recommendations: 
Management: For teeth 14 and 15, rubber dam isolation was applied, and temporary seals were removed. The pulp chamber was accessed, and the canal orifices were explored. Pulp extirpation was performed. GG drills were used to prepare the coronal third of the canal, and an 08# C file was used to determine the canal length, measured at 19mm with an apex locator. Canals were prepared using a large taper technique and machine files to F2. Ultrasonic irrigation with 1% sodium hypochlorite and distilled water was performed, alternating with saline, and the canals were dried. Calcium hydroxide paste was placed as a temporary seal.
Medical Instructions: Return if discomfort occurs. Avoid biting hard foods, maintain oral hygiene, and attend scheduled follow-ups.

Examination: Tooth 15 shows no percussion pain or gingival inflammation and has a temporary crown in place. Tooth 14 shows brown carious lesions on the cervical and proximal surfaces, probing to the dentin layer.
Radiographic Examination: 
Diagnosis: Deep caries of tooth 14 (K02.800x008).
Treatment Plan: 
Treatment Recommendations: 
Management: For tooth 15, the temporary crown and seal were removed. The abutment and restoration were cleaned, and the fit of the restoration was checked. Occlusion was adjusted, and the restoration was polished and permanently cemented. For tooth 14, carious dentin was removed using a chemical caries removal gel, reaching the deep dentin layer. The proximal surface was restored using a gingival retraction system, pulp protection, base lining, and resin filling. The filling was adjusted for occlusion and polished.
Medical Instructions: Avoid biting hard foods. Return if any discomfort occurs.

ground_truth:
Patient Record: Follow-up consultation. Diagnosis: Pulpitis. Treatment: Root canal therapy.
Examination: Tooth 24 shows ink-like discoloration on the distal proximal surface, with probe retention reaching deep dentin, and sensitivity to cold stimuli.
Radiographic Examination: 
Diagnosis: Pulpitis in tooth 24.
Treatment Plan: Root canal therapy for tooth 24.
Treatment Recommendations: 
Management: Tooth 24: The patient was informed about the condition and consented to treatment. Under rubber dam isolation, caries removal was performed using high-speed handpiece and slow-speed round bur, followed by pulp chamber opening, pulp extirpation, and canal negotiation. The canals were irrigated with saline, and canal length was determined (17, 19 mm) using an electronic apex locator. Nickel-titanium rotary files were used to prepare the canals to size F2. The canals were disinfected with 2.5% sodium hypochlorite and distilled water, and a trial fit of the master cone was taken. After irrigation, drying, and isolation, the canals were dried with paper points and obturated using SP and gutta-percha with vertical compaction. A post-operative radiograph confirmed proper filling. Post space preparation was done, and a fiber post was bonded. The tooth was prepared and refined for a crown. A silicone impression and bite registration were taken, and a temporary crown was placed.
Medical Instructions: Return for follow-up if any discomfort occurs.
Remarks:

Patient Record: Initial consultation. Diagnosis: deep caries. Treatment: resin crown restoration; resin filling; BP base lining, metal crown restoration.
Examination: [E] shows mesial caries with ink-stained changes, negative probing, and percussion. [D] shows distal caries, negative probing, and percussion, with X-ray showing deep shadow near the pulp. [A][A] shows mesial caries, negative probing, and percussion.
Radiographic Examination: 
Diagnosis: Deep caries on [A][A][0][DE].
Treatment Plan: [A][A]: Resin crown restoration. [E]: Resin filling. [D]: BP base lining, metal crown restoration.
Treatment Recommendations: 
Management: [A][A]: The patient was informed of the diagnosis, treatment plan, costs, and prognosis, and consented to the treatment. Local infiltration anesthesia with 4% articaine was administered, caries were removed near the pulp, and resin crowns were bonded. [E]: The patient was informed of the diagnosis, treatment plan, costs, and prognosis, and consented to the treatment. Local infiltration anesthesia with 4% articaine was administered, caries were removed, the cavity was prepared, isolated, dried, bonding agent applied, resin filling placed, and polished. [D]: The patient was informed of the diagnosis, treatment plan, costs, and prognosis, and consented to the treatment. Local infiltration anesthesia with 4% articaine was administered, caries were removed, partial pulp was removed, IROOT BP was placed over the pulp exposure, resin filling was placed, and a metal preformed crown was prepared and cemented.
Medical Instructions: Return for follow-up if discomfort occurs.

Examination: Teeth 13 and 14 are temporarily sealed, with normal mucosa in the periapical area and no percussion sensitivity.
Radiographic Examination: 
Diagnosis: Pulpitis in teeth 13 and 14 (K04.005).

Treatment Recommendations: 
Management: For teeth 13 and 14, temporary seals were removed, and the root canals were cleaned with 1% sodium hypochlorite and distilled water using ultrasonic agitation. Master cone fit was verified with a trial radiograph, and the main cone was sterilized for use. The canals were dried with paper points, and obturation was completed using sealer and gutta-percha cones. A post-operative radiograph confirmed proper filling. For tooth 14, a fiber post was placed, occlusion adjusted, and polished. Both teeth were prepared for crowns, impressions and bite registrations were taken, and temporary crowns were fabricated and placed.
Medical Instructions: Return if discomfort occurs. Avoid biting hard foods, maintain oral hygiene, and attend follow-up appointments as scheduled.

Patient Record: Follow-up consultation. Diagnosis: secondary caries (K02.800x001).
Examination: Tooth 36 shows loss of temporary material, with normal mucosa in the apical area and no significant gingival swelling. Percussion test is negative.
Radiographic Examination: 
Diagnosis: Secondary caries in tooth 36 (K02.800x001).
Treatment Plan: 
Treatment Recommendations: 
Management: For tooth 36, the tooth surface was polished, and the inlay was tried in. The inlay and tooth surface were treated with hydrofluoric acid etching for 90 seconds, rinsed, and dried. Ultrasonic cleaning with 95% alcohol for 90 seconds was performed, followed by drying. Silane coupling agent was applied and dried with hot air for 20 seconds. The tooth was etched, rinsed, dried, and isolated. Both the tooth and inlay were coated with bonding agent (Ivoclar Vivadent Variolink N system), and the inlay was cemented with dual-cure resin cement, followed by light curing. Occlusion was adjusted, polished, and photographed.
Medical Instructions: Advise the patient to avoid consuming excessively cold, hot, hard, or sticky foods. If any loosening or discomfort occurs, return for follow-up, scheduling an appointment by phone in advance.

Patient Record: Follow-up consultation. Diagnosis: Angle’s Class III malocclusion (K07.200x013). Treatment: Align and level the dental arches, flatten the Spee curve, correct intermaxillary relations, expand the dental arch, distalize molars, advance the maxillary arch, inhibit mandibular growth, increase anterior and posterior tooth height, perform interproximal enamel reduction. Treatment method: Invisible aligners.
Examination: Normal overbite and overjet. Skeletal Class III with a normal mandibular plane angle. Normal anterior open bite. Normal dental arch crowding. Normal occlusal plane. Maxillary arch is ovoid, mandibular arch is square. High palatal vault. Acceptable lingual frenulum attachment. Retrusive mandible. General oral hygiene is average, with normal oral mucosa, tongue, and speech. Normal upper and lower midlines. Normal maxilla and mandible. Alveolar bone is underdeveloped in both arches. Symmetrical face with concave profile and short facial height. Normal lip-tooth relationship with full lips.
Radiographic Examination: 
Diagnosis: Angle’s Class III malocclusion (K07.200x013).
Treatment Plan: Align and level the dental arches, flatten the Spee curve, correct intermaxillary relations, expand the dental arch, distalize molars, advance the maxillary arch, inhibit mandibular growth, increase anterior and posterior tooth height, perform interproximal enamel reduction. Treatment method: Invisible aligners.
Treatment Recommendations: 
Management: Photographs, oral scans, and radiographs were taken to formulate a treatment plan.
Medical Instructions: Return for follow-up if discomfort occurs and schedule regular check-ups.

Medical Instructions: Brush thoroughly twice daily, especially around the brackets. Use interdental brushes and floss to clean between teeth. Avoid sticky and hard foods that can damage the braces.
Remarks: The patient is motivated for treatment and understands the duration and commitment required for successful orthodontic correction.

Patient Record: Initial consultation. Diagnosis: Extrinsic dental pigmentation (K08.2).
Examination: All teeth show dark brown to black pigmentation, primarily on the occlusal and interproximal surfaces. Gingiva appears slightly erythematous without signs of inflammation or bleeding. No mobility or tenderness in the teeth. No visible caries or restorations.
Radiographic Examination: Panoramic and periapical radiographs show normal bone levels and tooth structures. No evidence of underlying pathology or bone loss.
Diagnosis: Extrinsic dental pigmentation affecting all teeth (ICD-10: K08.2).
Treatment Plan: 
1. Professional dental cleaning to remove pigmentation and calculus.
2. Application of topical fluoride to prevent demineralization.
3. Oral hygiene instruction and recommendation for regular dental check-ups.
Treatment Recommendations: Maintain good oral hygiene with regular brushing and flossing. Use desensitizing toothpaste if sensitivity occurs. Schedule biannual dental cleanings.
Management: Ultrasonic scaling and manual curettage were performed to remove pigmentation and calculus. The patient's teeth were polished with pumice and water. Topical fluoride varnish was applied.
Medical Instructions: Avoid staining agents like coffee, tea, and tobacco. Brush twice daily with fluoride toothpaste. Floss daily and use an antimicrobial mouthwash. Return for a follow-up in six months.
Remarks: The pigmentation appears to be extrinsic, likely due to dietary factors or smoking. Regular professional cleanings will help maintain dental health.
